title: Lesson 230
transcript: One of the most important aspects of protecting assets is that in most organizations now, assets need to be kept around for a very long time. In fact, if we're talking specifically about data and information, There could be a requirement for organizations to keep information around for a very, very long time. In fact, here's an example, health organizations. Health organizations may have a need to keep that health information around for over 100 years. If you imagine a baby that has a medical condition, for example, that health organization has to keep that information around for the life of that baby plus 20 or 30 years. And so that could be an extensive period of time. The problem from a security perspective is how do you protect that information for over 100 years. In fact, what guarantee is there that we're going to be able to read that information 100 years from now? These are some of the challenges that companies face in protecting their assets, specifically, for example, information. And so how do we keep that information around for a very long time? First of all, we have to understand the requirements. And this is part of what any company would consider to be their compliance requirements. A lot of it will be driven by laws. regulations, in some cases industry standards, but also in some cases it could be company policies management would dictate or understand that we need to keep information or records around for a very long time. But that requires security to be involved now and make sure that we have proper security controls throughout that long retention period, which is typically taken care of through our archiving policies. Archiving policies is how we keep information around for a very long time. Now data archiving then, these are some of the questions that you might see. What drives the data archiving requirements of an organization are going to be things related to, again, compliance requirements that are dictated by laws and regulations. and industry standards and company policies. But to keep information around for a hundred years requires us to answer some pretty good questions. Like for example, what media do you store it on? that's going to allow us to not only keep it around for a very long time, but also possibly access it throughout that long retention period, but also how to protect it. Certain media is easier to protect than others. For example, simply printing it and throwing it in a box shipping it off to Iron Mountain may not give that information the level of protection that it requires based on compliance requirements. And so if security is something else that we have to think about throughout that long retention requirement. What about availability requirements? Subjects for example have through privacy laws the ability to request that information that you're keeping on them and facilitating that is something that needs to be done by the organization as well. If someone says, I need access to these records, you can't say, for example, well, it's going to take us three weeks to be able to get those back because First of all, we don't know where they are or possibly we don't know what format they're in and whether we can read them. So understanding the availability requirements and how certain subjects or custodians or owners may need access to that information at a certain point throughout that retention requirement. is very important as well. The retention period itself again is dictated by typically laws and regulations. And so what we need to have is an overall policy that talks about retention and archiving But that needs to reflect on things that you see on the slide here. You know, how long do we keep that information around for? How is it secured through that entire period? And how do we facilitate access to it when people need access to it?
